  • 2-1) Merge Intervals (LC 56) — Sort + Merge
  • 2-2) Non-overlapping Intervals (LC 435) — Greedy Interval Scheduling
  • 2-3) Insert Interval (LC 57) — Linear Scan + Merge
  • 2-4) Meeting Rooms II (LC 253) — Min-Heap on End Times

  • 2-7) Interval List Intersections (LC 986) — Two Pointers
  • 2-8) Remove Covered Intervals (LC 1288) — Sort + Greedy
  • 2-9) Video Stitching (LC 1024) — Greedy Interval Cover
  • 2-10) Maximum Profit in Job Scheduling (LC 1235) — DP + Binary Search
  • 2-11) My Calendar I (LC 729) — TreeMap Overlap Check
  • 2-12) Meeting Rooms I (LC 252) — Sort + Adjacent Check

Intervals

Overview

Intervals are problems involving ranges of values, typically represented as [start, end] pairs, requiring operations like merging overlapping ranges, finding intersections, or scheduling non-overlapping events.

Key Properties

  • Time Complexity: O(n log n) for sorting + O(n) for processing = O(n log n) overall
  • Space Complexity: O(1) to O(n) depending on output requirements
  • Core Idea: Sort intervals by start time, then process linearly to handle overlaps
  • When to Use: Problems involving ranges, scheduling, calendar management, resource allocation

Core Algorithm Steps

  1. Sort intervals by start time (occasionally by end time for greedy problems)
  2. Process sequentially to identify overlaps or non-overlaps
  3. Apply merge/remove strategy based on problem requirements
  4. Handle edge cases like empty intervals or single intervals

When to Use Interval Algorithms

  • Merge overlapping ranges: Calendar conflicts, memory allocation
  • Scheduling optimization: Meeting rooms, task assignment
  • Range queries: Time series data, genomic sequences
  • Resource management: Bandwidth allocation, CPU scheduling

References

  • labuladong: Interval Merge
  • labuladong: Interval Overlap
  • Visualization Explanation

1) Problem Categories

Pattern 1: Interval Merging

  • Description: Combine overlapping intervals into single merged intervals
  • Examples: LC 56 (Merge Intervals), LC 57 (Insert Interval)
  • Recognition: “Merge”, “combine”, “overlapping intervals”
  • Sorting: By start time (ascending)

Pattern 2: Interval Scheduling (Greedy)

  • Description: Find maximum non-overlapping intervals or minimum intervals to remove
  • Examples: LC 435 (Non-overlapping Intervals), LC 452 (Minimum Arrows)
  • Recognition: “Maximum”, “minimum”, “non-overlapping”, “remove”
  • Sorting: By end time (ascending) for greedy approach

Pattern 3: Interval Intersection

  • Description: Find common time slots or overlapping regions between interval lists
  • Examples: LC 986 (Interval List Intersections), LC 1288 (Remove Covered Intervals)
  • Recognition: “Intersection”, “overlap”, “common”, “covered”
  • Sorting: By start time, process two pointers

Pattern 4: Interval Point Coverage

  • Description: Determine points that can cover multiple intervals or find gaps
  • Examples: LC 452 (Minimum Arrows), LC 1024 (Video Stitching)
  • Recognition: “Cover”, “points”, “arrows”, “minimum coverage”
  • Sorting: By start or end time depending on strategy

Pattern 5: Meeting Room Scheduling

  • Description: Determine meeting room requirements or check scheduling conflicts
  • Examples: LC 252 (Meeting Rooms), LC 253 (Meeting Rooms II)
  • Recognition: “Meeting”, “conference”, “rooms”, “schedule conflicts”
  • Sorting: By start time, use priority queue for room management

Pattern 6: Calendar and Booking

  • Description: Handle calendar bookings with conflict detection and resolution
  • Examples: LC 729 (My Calendar I), LC 731 (My Calendar II), LC 732 (My Calendar III)
  • Recognition: “Calendar”, “booking”, “double booking”, “k-booking”
  • Sorting: Maintain sorted intervals, binary search for insertion

2) Templates & Algorithms

Template Comparison Table

Template Type Use Case Sorting Strategy When to Use
Merge Template Combine overlapping intervals Sort by start time LC 56, 57, merging problems
Greedy Template Maximum non-overlapping Sort by end time LC 435, 452, scheduling optimization
Two Pointer Template Intersection/comparison Sort both lists by start LC 986, comparing interval lists
Priority Queue Template Resource management Sort by start, heap by end LC 253, meeting room problems
Binary Search Template Calendar/booking Maintain sorted order LC 729-732, dynamic interval insertion

Universal Interval Template

1def solve_interval_problem(intervals):
2    """
3    Universal template for interval problems
4    """
5    # Step 1: Handle edge cases
6    if not intervals or len(intervals) <= 1:
7        return intervals
8    
9    # Step 2: Sort intervals (by start time or end time based on problem)
10    intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])  # Sort by start time
11    # intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])  # Sort by end time for greedy problems
12    
13    # Step 3: Initialize result
14    result = []
15    
16    # Step 4: Process intervals sequentially
17    for current in intervals:
18        # Step 5: Check overlap condition with last processed interval
19        if not result or no_overlap_condition(result[-1], current):
20            result.append(current)
21        else:
22            # Step 6: Handle overlap (merge, count, or remove)
23            handle_overlap(result, current)
24    
25    return result
26
27def no_overlap_condition(prev, curr):
28    """Check if two intervals don't overlap"""
29    return prev[1] < curr[0]  # prev ends before curr starts
30
31def handle_overlap(result, current):
32    """Handle overlapping intervals based on problem type"""
33    # For merging: extend the last interval
34    result[-1][1] = max(result[-1][1], current[1])
35    # For counting: increment counter
36    # For removal: choose which interval to keep

Specific Templates

Template 1: Interval Merging (LC 56, 57)

1def merge_intervals(intervals):
2    """
3    Merge overlapping intervals
4    Time: O(n log n), Space: O(n)
5    """
6    if not intervals:
7        return []
8    
9    # Sort by start time
10    intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])
11    merged = [intervals[0]]
12    
13    for current in intervals[1:]:
14        last = merged[-1]
15        
16        # No overlap: add current interval
17        if last[1] < current[0]:
18            merged.append(current)
19        # Overlap: merge intervals
20        else:
21            last[1] = max(last[1], current[1])
22    
23    return merged
24
25# Java version
26public int[][] merge(int[][] intervals) {
27    if (intervals.length <= 1) return intervals;
28    
29    Arrays.sort(intervals, (a, b) -> Integer.compare(a[0], b[0]));
30    List<int[]> merged = new ArrayList<>();
31    
32    for (int[] current : intervals) {
33        if (merged.isEmpty() || merged.get(merged.size() - 1)[1] < current[0]) {
34            merged.add(current);
35        } else {
36            merged.get(merged.size() - 1)[1] = Math.max(
37                merged.get(merged.size() - 1)[1], current[1]
38            );
39        }
40    }
41    
42    return merged.toArray(new int[merged.size()][]);
43}

Template 2: Greedy Scheduling (LC 435, 452)

1def min_intervals_to_remove(intervals):
2    """
3    Find minimum intervals to remove for non-overlapping set
4    Time: O(n log n), Space: O(1)
5    """
6    if not intervals:
7        return 0
8    
9    # Sort by end time (greedy strategy)
10    intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])
11    
12    count = 0
13    prev_end = intervals[0][1]
14    
15    for i in range(1, len(intervals)):
16        # Overlap detected
17        if intervals[i][0] < prev_end:
18            count += 1  # Remove current interval
19        else:
20            prev_end = intervals[i][1]  # Update end time
21    
22    return count
23
24# Java version
25public int eraseOverlapIntervals(int[][] intervals) {
26    if (intervals.length <= 1) return 0;
27    
28    Arrays.sort(intervals, (a, b) -> Integer.compare(a[1], b[1]));
29    
30    int count = 0;
31    int prevEnd = intervals[0][1];
32    
33    for (int i = 1; i < intervals.length; i++) {
34        if (intervals[i][0] < prevEnd) {
35            count++;
36        } else {
37            prevEnd = intervals[i][1];
38        }
39    }
40    
41    return count;
42}

Template 3: Two Pointer Intersection (LC 986)

1def interval_intersection(firstList, secondList):
2    """
3    Find intersection of two interval lists
4    Time: O(m + n), Space: O(min(m, n))
5    """
6    result = []
7    i = j = 0
8    
9    while i < len(firstList) and j < len(secondList):
10        # Find intersection
11        start = max(firstList[i][0], secondList[j][0])
12        end = min(firstList[i][1], secondList[j][1])
13        
14        # Valid intersection
15        if start <= end:
16            result.append([start, end])
17        
18        # Move pointer of interval that ends first
19        if firstList[i][1] < secondList[j][1]:
20            i += 1
21        else:
22            j += 1
23    
24    return result

Template 4: Meeting Rooms with Priority Queue (LC 253)

1import heapq
2
3def min_meeting_rooms(intervals):
4    """
5    Find minimum meeting rooms required
6    Time: O(n log n), Space: O(n)
7    """
8    if not intervals:
9        return 0
10    
11    # Sort by start time
12    intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])
13    
14    # Min heap to track end times
15    heap = []
16    
17    for start, end in intervals:
18        # If earliest meeting ends before current starts
19        if heap and heap[0] <= start:
20            heapq.heappop(heap)
21        
22        # Add current meeting's end time
23        heapq.heappush(heap, end)
24    
25    return len(heap)
26
27# Java version
28public int minMeetingRooms(int[][] intervals) {
29    if (intervals.length == 0) return 0;
30    
31    Arrays.sort(intervals, (a, b) -> Integer.compare(a[0], b[0]));
32    PriorityQueue<Integer> heap = new PriorityQueue<>();
33    
34    for (int[] interval : intervals) {
35        if (!heap.isEmpty() && heap.peek() <= interval[0]) {
36            heap.poll();
37        }
38        heap.offer(interval[1]);
39    }
40    
41    return heap.size();
42}

Template 5: Calendar Booking (LC 729)

1class MyCalendar:
2    """
3    Calendar with overlap detection using binary search
4    Time: O(log n) per booking, Space: O(n)
5    """
6    def __init__(self):
7        self.bookings = []
8    
9    def book(self, start, end):
10        # Binary search for insertion position
11        left, right = 0, len(self.bookings)
12        
13        while left < right:
14            mid = (left + right) // 2
15            if self.bookings[mid][1] <= start:
16                left = mid + 1
17            else:
18                right = mid
19        
20        # Check overlap with neighbors
21        if left > 0 and self.bookings[left - 1][1] > start:
22            return False
23        if left < len(self.bookings) and self.bookings[left][0] < end:
24            return False
25        
26        # No overlap, insert booking
27        self.bookings.insert(left, [start, end])
28        return True

3) Problems by Pattern

Merging Pattern Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty Template
Merge Intervals 56 Sort by start, merge overlaps Medium Merge Template
Insert Interval 57 Insert and merge Medium Merge Template
Summary Ranges 228 Consecutive number ranges Easy Merge Template
Data Stream as Disjoint Intervals 352 TreeMap/SortedDict Hard Merge Template
Merge Similar Items 2363 Merge by weight Easy Merge Template

Greedy Scheduling Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty Template
Non-overlapping Intervals 435 Sort by end, greedy removal Medium Greedy Template
Minimum Arrows to Burst Balloons 452 Sort by end, count arrows Medium Greedy Template
Maximum Length of Pair Chain 646 Sort by second element Medium Greedy Template
Activity Selection Problem - Classic greedy algorithm Medium Greedy Template
Car Pooling 1094 Timeline + capacity Medium Greedy Template

Intersection & Coverage Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty Template
Interval List Intersections 986 Two pointers Medium Two Pointer Template
Remove Covered Intervals 1288 Sort and filter Medium Merge Template
Find Right Interval 436 Binary search Medium Binary Search
Employee Free Time 759 Merge + find gaps Hard Merge Template
Video Stitching 1024 Greedy coverage Medium Greedy Template

Meeting Room & Scheduling Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty Template
Meeting Rooms 252 Sort and check conflicts Easy Basic Template
Meeting Rooms II 253 Priority queue Medium Priority Queue Template
Meeting Scheduler 1229 Two pointers + duration Medium Two Pointer Template
Minimum Time to Make Rope Colorful 1578 Consecutive intervals Medium Greedy Template
Course Schedule III 630 Priority queue + greedy Hard Priority Queue Template

Calendar & Booking Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty Template
My Calendar I 729 Sorted list + binary search Medium Calendar Template
My Calendar II 731 Double booking detection Medium Calendar Template
My Calendar III 732 K-booking with timeline Hard Calendar Template
Exam Room 855 Max gap maintenance Medium Binary Search
Range Module 715 Segment tree/intervals Hard Advanced Template

Advanced Interval Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty Template
Falling Squares 699 Coordinate compression Hard Advanced Template
The Skyline Problem 218 Sweep line + priority queue Hard Advanced Template
Rectangle Area II 850 Coordinate compression Hard Advanced Template
Perfect Rectangle 391 Area calculation + validation Hard Advanced Template
Count Integers in Intervals 2276 Dynamic intervals Hard Advanced Template

4) Pattern Selection Strategy

Decision Framework Flowchart

1Problem Analysis for Interval Problems:
2
31. Are you merging overlapping intervals?
4   ├── YES → Use Merge Template (LC 56, 57)
5   │   ├── Single interval insertion? → Insert Interval Template
6   │   └── Multiple overlaps? → Standard Merge Template
7   └── NO → Continue to 2
8
92. Are you finding maximum non-overlapping intervals?
10   ├── YES → Use Greedy Template (LC 435, 452)
11   │   ├── Sort by end time
12   │   └── Greedy selection strategy
13   └── NO → Continue to 3
14
153. Are you finding intersections between interval lists?
16   ├── YES → Use Two Pointer Template (LC 986)
17   │   ├── Two sorted lists? → Standard Two Pointer
18   │   └── Multiple lists? → Merge then process
19   └── NO → Continue to 4
20
214. Are you managing meeting rooms or resources?
22   ├── YES → Use Priority Queue Template (LC 253)
23   │   ├── Count resources needed? → Min heap approach
24   │   └── Check availability? → Sort + scan
25   └── NO → Continue to 5
26
275. Are you handling dynamic bookings/calendar?
28   ├── YES → Use Calendar Template (LC 729-732)
29   │   ├── Single booking? → Binary search insertion
30   │   ├── Double booking allowed? → Two lists approach
31   │   └── K-booking? → Timeline/sweep line
32   └── NO → Consider Advanced Templates
33
346. Advanced cases (Skyline, Rectangles, etc.)
35   ├── Coordinate compression needed?
36   ├── Sweep line algorithm required?
37   └── Segment tree for range operations?

Template Selection Guide

Quick Decision Tree:

  1. Overlap Detection: prev[1] >= curr[0] (assuming sorted by start)
  2. Merge Strategy: Extend prev[1] = max(prev[1], curr[1])
  3. Greedy Strategy: Sort by end time, keep earliest ending
  4. Resource Management: Use min heap for end times
  5. Dynamic Insertion: Maintain sorted order with binary search

5) Key Patterns & Overlap Detection

Overlap Detection Methods

Method 1: After Sorting by Start Time

1def has_overlap(interval1, interval2):
2    """Check if two intervals overlap (sorted by start)"""
3    return interval1[1] > interval2[0]

Method 2: General Case (Any Order)

1def has_overlap(interval1, interval2):
2    """Check if two intervals overlap (any order)"""
3    start1, end1 = interval1
4    start2, end2 = interval2
5    return start1 < end2 and start2 < end1

Overlap Visualization

1Case 1 - No Overlap:
2|----| interval1
3        |----| interval2
4
5Case 2 - Overlap:
6|-------|
7    |-------|
8
9Case 3 - Complete Overlap:
10|-----------|
11   |-----|

Common Interval Operations

1def merge_two_intervals(a, b):
2    """Merge two overlapping intervals"""
3    return [min(a[0], b[0]), max(a[1], b[1])]
4
5def interval_length(interval):
6    """Calculate interval length"""
7    return interval[1] - interval[0]
8
9def intervals_intersection(a, b):
10    """Find intersection of two intervals"""
11    start = max(a[0], b[0])
12    end = min(a[1], b[1])
13    return [start, end] if start <= end else None
14
15def point_in_interval(point, interval):
16    """Check if point is in interval"""
17    return interval[0] <= point <= interval[1]

6) Summary & Quick Reference

Complexity Quick Reference

Operation Time Space Notes
Sort intervals O(n log n) O(1) Essential first step
Merge overlapping O(n) O(n) After sorting
Find intersections O(m + n) O(min(m,n)) Two pointer approach
Meeting rooms O(n log n) O(n) Priority queue for end times
Calendar booking O(log n) O(n) Binary search per insertion
Greedy scheduling O(n log n) O(1) Sort by end time

Template Quick Reference

Template Pattern Key Code
Merge Overlapping intervals if last[1] < curr[0]: append else: merge
Greedy Non-overlapping max sort(key=end); if curr[0] >= prev[1]: count++
Two Pointer List intersection start=max(starts), end=min(ends)
Priority Queue Resource management heappush(end_time); if heap[0] <= start: heappop
Binary Search Dynamic insertion bisect.insort or custom binary search

Common Patterns & Tricks

Pattern 1: Merge Overlapping

1# Standard merging after sorting
2intervals.sort()
3merged = [intervals[0]]
4for curr in intervals[1:]:
5    if merged[-1][1] < curr[0]:
6        merged.append(curr)
7    else:
8        merged[-1][1] = max(merged[-1][1], curr[1])

Pattern 2: Greedy Selection

1# Sort by end time for optimal selection
2intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])
3count = 1
4prev_end = intervals[0][1]
5for start, end in intervals[1:]:
6    if start >= prev_end:
7        count += 1
8        prev_end = end

Pattern 3: Timeline Events

1# Convert intervals to events for sweep line
2events = []
3for start, end in intervals:
4    events.append((start, 1))    # start event
5    events.append((end, -1))     # end event
6events.sort()

Problem-Solving Steps

  1. Identify Pattern: Merging, scheduling, intersection, or resource management?
  2. Choose Sorting Strategy: By start time (merging) or end time (greedy)
  3. Select Template: Use appropriate template from above
  4. Handle Edge Cases: Empty arrays, single intervals, identical intervals
  5. Optimize: Consider space optimization for large datasets

Common Mistakes & Tips

🚫 Common Mistakes:

  • Wrong sorting order: Sorting by start when should sort by end (greedy problems)
  • Off-by-one errors: Using <= vs < in overlap conditions
  • Edge case handling: Not checking empty arrays or single intervals
  • Merge logic errors: Forgetting to update both start and end during merge
  • Greedy strategy confusion: Not understanding why sorting by end time works
  • Space complexity: Creating unnecessary intermediate data structures

✅ Best Practices:

  • Always sort first: Most interval problems require sorted input
  • Clear overlap definition: Define overlap condition clearly before coding
  • Use appropriate template: Match template to problem pattern
  • Test edge cases: Empty input, single interval, identical intervals
  • Visualize examples: Draw intervals to understand overlap patterns
  • Choose right sorting key: Start time for merging, end time for greedy

Interview Tips

  1. Start with examples: Draw intervals on paper to visualize
  2. Clarify edge cases: What about empty intervals? Point intervals?
  3. Explain sorting choice: Why sorting by start/end time?
  4. Walk through algorithm: Show merge/greedy logic step by step
  5. Optimize incrementally: Start with working solution, then optimize
  6. Practice common patterns: Master the 5 main templates above
  7. Time complexity analysis: Always explain O(n log n) sorting + O(n) processing

Data Structure Conversion Tricks

List to Array (Java)

1List<int[]> result = new ArrayList<>();
2// ... populate result
3return result.toArray(new int[result.size()][]);

Efficient Merging in Python

1# Using list comprehension for functional style
2def merge_intervals(intervals):
3    intervals.sort()
4    result = [intervals[0]]
5    [result.append(curr) if result[-1][1] < curr[0] 
6     else result[-1].__setitem__(1, max(result[-1][1], curr[1]))
7     for curr in intervals[1:]]
8    return result

Related Topics

  • Greedy Algorithms: Interval scheduling optimization
  • Binary Search: Calendar booking and insertion problems
  • Priority Queue: Meeting room and resource management
  • Two Pointers: Intersection and comparison problems
  • Sweep Line: Advanced problems like skyline and rectangles
  • Segment Trees: Range updates and queries on intervals

LC Examples

2-1) Merge Intervals (LC 56) — Sort + Merge

Sort by start time; merge overlapping intervals by comparing with last merged.

1// LC 56 - Merge Intervals
2// IDEA: Sort by start, merge when current.start <= last.end
3// time = O(N log N), space = O(N)
4public int[][] merge(int[][] intervals) {
5    Arrays.sort(intervals, (a, b) -> a[0] - b[0]);
6    List<int[]> merged = new ArrayList<>();
7    for (int[] interval : intervals) {
8        if (merged.isEmpty() || merged.get(merged.size()-1)[1] < interval[0]) {
9            merged.add(interval);
10        } else {
11            merged.get(merged.size()-1)[1] = Math.max(merged.get(merged.size()-1)[1], interval[1]);
12        }
13    }
14    return merged.toArray(new int[merged.size()][]);
15}

2-2) Non-overlapping Intervals (LC 435) — Greedy Interval Scheduling

Sort by end time; greedily keep intervals that end earliest to minimize removals.

1// LC 435 - Non-overlapping Intervals
2// IDEA: Greedy — sort by end, count overlapping intervals to remove
3// time = O(N log N), space = O(1)
4public int eraseOverlapIntervals(int[][] intervals) {
5    Arrays.sort(intervals, (a, b) -> a[1] - b[1]);
6    int removals = 0, prevEnd = Integer.MIN_VALUE;
7    for (int[] interval : intervals) {
8        if (interval[0] < prevEnd) {
9            removals++;   // overlap: remove current (keep the one ending earlier)
10        } else {
11            prevEnd = interval[1];
12        }
13    }
14    return removals;
15}

2-3) Insert Interval (LC 57) — Linear Scan + Merge

Insert new interval and merge all overlapping intervals in one pass.

1// LC 57 - Insert Interval
2// IDEA: Three phases — add non-overlapping left, merge overlapping, add right
3// time = O(N), space = O(N)
4public int[][] insert(int[][] intervals, int[] newInterval) {
5    List<int[]> result = new ArrayList<>();
6    int i = 0, n = intervals.length;
7    // Phase 1: add all intervals that end before newInterval starts
8    while (i < n && intervals[i][1] < newInterval[0]) result.add(intervals[i++]);
9    // Phase 2: merge overlapping intervals
10    while (i < n && intervals[i][0] <= newInterval[1]) {
11        newInterval[0] = Math.min(newInterval[0], intervals[i][0]);
12        newInterval[1] = Math.max(newInterval[1], intervals[i][1]);
13        i++;
14    }
15    result.add(newInterval);
16    // Phase 3: add remaining intervals
17    while (i < n) result.add(intervals[i++]);
18    return result.toArray(new int[result.size()][]);
19}

2-4) Meeting Rooms II (LC 253) — Min-Heap on End Times

Sort by start; heap tracks earliest ending room — reuse if room ends before next meeting.

1// LC 253 - Meeting Rooms II
2// IDEA: Sort by start; min-heap of end times — reuse room if heap.peek() <= start
3// time = O(N log N), space = O(N)
4public int minMeetingRooms(int[][] intervals) {
5    Arrays.sort(intervals, (a, b) -> a[0] - b[0]);
6    PriorityQueue<Integer> heap = new PriorityQueue<>();
7    for (int[] iv : intervals) {
8        if (!heap.isEmpty() && heap.peek() <= iv[0]) heap.poll();
9        heap.offer(iv[1]);
10    }
11    return heap.size();
12}

2-5) Minimum Number of Arrows to Burst Balloons (LC 452) — Greedy

Sort by end; one arrow at interval’s end bursts all overlapping; advance when gap appears.

1// LC 452 - Minimum Number of Arrows to Burst Balloons
2// IDEA: Greedy — sort by end; new arrow only when next start > current end
3// time = O(N log N), space = O(1)
4public int findMinArrowShots(int[][] points) {
5    Arrays.sort(points, (a, b) -> Integer.compare(a[1], b[1]));
6    int arrows = 1, end = points[0][1];
7    for (int i = 1; i < points.length; i++)
8        if (points[i][0] > end) { arrows++; end = points[i][1]; }
9    return arrows;
10}

2-6) Non-Overlapping Intervals (LC 435) — Greedy

Sort by end; keep interval with earliest end greedily; count how many must be removed.

1// LC 435 - Non-Overlapping Intervals
2// IDEA: Greedy — sort by end; skip (remove) overlapping intervals
3// time = O(N log N), space = O(1)
4public int eraseOverlapIntervals(int[][] intervals) {
5    Arrays.sort(intervals, (a, b) -> a[1] - b[1]);
6    int removals = 0, end = Integer.MIN_VALUE;
7    for (int[] iv : intervals) {
8        if (iv[0] >= end) end = iv[1];
9        else removals++;
10    }
11    return removals;
12}

2-7) Interval List Intersections (LC 986) — Two Pointers

Advance the pointer whose interval ends first; record overlap when ranges intersect.

1// LC 986 - Interval List Intersections
2// IDEA: Two pointers — compute intersection, advance pointer with smaller end
3// time = O(M+N), space = O(M+N)
4public int[][] intervalIntersection(int[][] A, int[][] B) {
5    List<int[]> res = new ArrayList<>();
6    int i = 0, j = 0;
7    while (i < A.length && j < B.length) {
8        int lo = Math.max(A[i][0], B[j][0]);
9        int hi = Math.min(A[i][1], B[j][1]);
10        if (lo <= hi) res.add(new int[]{lo, hi});
11        if (A[i][1] < B[j][1]) i++;
12        else j++;
13    }
14    return res.toArray(new int[res.size()][]);
15}

2-8) Remove Covered Intervals (LC 1288) — Sort + Greedy

Sort by start asc, end desc; interval is covered if its end ≤ current max end.

1// LC 1288 - Remove Covered Intervals
2// IDEA: Sort start ASC, end DESC; count intervals not covered by running maxEnd
3// time = O(N log N), space = O(1)
4public int removeCoveredIntervals(int[][] intervals) {
5    Arrays.sort(intervals, (a, b) -> a[0] != b[0] ? a[0] - b[0] : b[1] - a[1]);
6    int count = 0, maxEnd = 0;
7    for (int[] iv : intervals)
8        if (iv[1] > maxEnd) { count++; maxEnd = iv[1]; }
9    return count;
10}

2-9) Video Stitching (LC 1024) — Greedy Interval Cover

Sort by start; at each frontier pick the clip extending coverage the furthest.

1// LC 1024 - Video Stitching
2// IDEA: Greedy — at current end, pick clip reaching farthest next position
3// time = O(N log N), space = O(1)
4public int videoStitching(int[][] clips, int time) {
5    Arrays.sort(clips, (a, b) -> a[0] - b[0]);
6    int count = 0, curEnd = 0, farthest = 0, i = 0;
7    while (i < clips.length && curEnd < time) {
8        while (i < clips.length && clips[i][0] <= curEnd)
9            farthest = Math.max(farthest, clips[i++][1]);
10        if (farthest == curEnd) return -1;
11        curEnd = farthest;
12        count++;
13    }
14    return curEnd >= time ? count : -1;
15}

2-10) Maximum Profit in Job Scheduling (LC 1235) — DP + Binary Search

Sort jobs by end; dp[i] = max profit using first i jobs; binary search for last non-conflicting job.

1// LC 1235 - Maximum Profit in Job Scheduling
2// IDEA: Sort by end; DP + binary search for latest non-overlapping job
3// time = O(N log N), space = O(N)
4public int jobScheduling(int[] startTime, int[] endTime, int[] profit) {
5    int n = startTime.length;
6    int[][] jobs = new int[n][3];
7    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) jobs[i] = new int[]{endTime[i], startTime[i], profit[i]};
8    Arrays.sort(jobs, (a, b) -> a[0] - b[0]);
9    int[] dp = new int[n + 1];
10    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
11        int lo = 0, hi = i;
12        while (lo < hi) {
13            int mid = (lo + hi + 1) / 2;
14            if (jobs[mid-1][0] <= jobs[i][1]) lo = mid;
15            else hi = mid - 1;
16        }
17        dp[i+1] = Math.max(dp[i], dp[lo] + jobs[i][2]);
18    }
19    return dp[n];
20}

2-11) My Calendar I (LC 729) — TreeMap Overlap Check

TreeMap floor/ceiling gives O(log N) overlap detection per booking.

1// LC 729 - My Calendar I
2// IDEA: TreeMap — O(log N) overlap check with floorKey / ceilingKey
3// time = O(log N) per booking, space = O(N)
4class MyCalendar {
5    TreeMap<Integer, Integer> cal = new TreeMap<>();
6    public boolean book(int start, int end) {
7        Integer prev = cal.floorKey(start), next = cal.ceilingKey(start);
8        if ((prev == null || cal.get(prev) <= start) && (next == null || next >= end)) {
9            cal.put(start, end);
10            return true;
11        }
12        return false;
13    }
14}

2-12) Meeting Rooms I (LC 252) — Sort + Adjacent Check

Sort by start time; if any meeting starts before previous ends, overlap exists.

1// LC 252 - Meeting Rooms
2// IDEA: Sort by start; adjacent overlap check
3// time = O(N log N), space = O(1)
4public boolean canAttendMeetings(int[][] intervals) {
5    Arrays.sort(intervals, (a, b) -> a[0] - b[0]);
6    for (int i = 1; i < intervals.length; i++)
7        if (intervals[i][0] < intervals[i-1][1]) return false;
8    return true;
9}
← Back to Cheat Sheets Edit on GitHub →
← HeapIterator →
